text: In this Artemis 2 image, the sun is positioned behind the Earth (on the side opposite the Moon and spacecraft), roughly aligned with the top of the frame where Earth appears. That's why Earth shows only a thin crescent—the side facing the camera is mostly its night side. The Moon's lit craters are consistent with sunlight coming from that same direction. No inconsistency with the eclipse geometry.

I thoroughly enjoyed watching the #ArtemisII #crew's #return to #Earth, as it provides a sense of the #timeline that's missing when you only watch a retrospective summary of events.
I was impressed by the view of Earth approaching with its seemingly even visible #atmosphere. The feeling of witnessing exactly what was happening before the astronauts' eyes at the same time gave me the sense of actually participating. Although my actual ticket as a #virtualguest primarily had the advantage of receiving regular email updates on the mission's preparations.

When the officially anticipated loss of contact with the crew began after the #capsule #reentered Earth's atmosphere, six minutes suddenly seemed incredibly long to me, just as they probably did to the #astronauts. The subsequent alternating deployment of different #parachutes to reduce the capsule's speed was so perfectly executed that I felt like I was watching an art installation. After landing in the Pacific #Ocean, the astronauts, like the onlookers, seemed to need #patience above all else. It took a long time for the crew to assemble an ingenious system of two structures: one that enclosed and stabilized the capsule and made its surroundings accessible, and a second structure built like a small island where the Artemis crew finally waited to be #airlifted by #helicopter to a nearby ship.

Now, of course, I'm eager to learn what interesting #researchdata the crew brought back besides more breathtaking photos.

Artemis 2: The First Crewed Journey Around the Moon in Over Half a Century
In a historic step that returns humanity to lunar exploration, NASA launched the Artemis II mission on April 1, 2026 from Kennedy Space Center in Florida. This was the first crewed flight in the Artemis program and the first time humans left low Earth orbit since Apollo 17 in 1972. The mission concluded successfully on April 10, 2026, with the Orion spacecraft splashing down in the Pacific Ocean off the coast of San Diego.2
Mission Objectives and Significance
Artemis II aimed to test all systems of the Orion spacecraft in deep space with a human crew onboard, ahead of future missions that will include landing on the Moon. The spacecraft did not land on the lunar surface but performed a “flyby,” looping around the Moon—including views of the far side that humans had not seen in this modern context.
Key achievements included:
•Breaking the Apollo 13 record for the farthest distance humans have traveled from Earth (reaching approximately 406,773 km).
•Testing Orion’s heat shield during high-speed re-entry, where temperatures reached half the surface temperature of the Sun.
•Capturing scientific observations and images of the Moon and Earth.
•Verifying life support, propulsion, and navigation systems during the roughly 10-day journey.17
This mission is a critical stepping stone for the ambitious Artemis program, which seeks to return humans to the Moon’s surface (starting with Artemis III), establish a sustainable human presence there, and eventually pave the way for Mars exploration.
The Historic Crew
The four-astronaut crew represented a new era of diversity:
•Reid Wiseman (NASA) – Mission Commander.
•Victor Glover (NASA) – Pilot; the first Black astronaut to travel beyond low Earth orbit.
•Christina Koch (NASA) – Mission Specialist; the first woman to fly on a lunar mission.
•Jeremy Hansen (Canadian Space Agency) – Mission Specialist; the first non-American astronaut on a lunar flight.
The crew named their spacecraft Integrity, symbolizing the values they represent.
Main Mission Phases
1Launch — On April 1, 2026, at approximately 6:35 p.m. EDT, using NASA’s powerful Space Launch System (SLS) rocket from Launch Complex 39B.
2Earth Orbit and Translunar Injection — Initial orbits around Earth followed by a burn to send Orion toward the Moon.
3Lunar Flyby — On April 6, 2026, the spacecraft looped around the far side of the Moon, coming within thousands of kilometers of its surface for observations and photography.
4Return to Earth — High-speed atmospheric re-entry and parachute-assisted splashdown in the Pacific Ocean on April 10 at about 8:07 p.m. EDT. The crew was safely recovered aboard the USS John P. Murtha.6
The mission successfully tested the heat shield and life support systems, despite minor issues such as a temporary urine vent blockage (which was partially resolved by heating the system).
Difference Between Artemis II and Artemis I
•Artemis I (2022): An uncrewed test flight that validated the SLS rocket and Orion spacecraft.
•Artemis II: The first crewed test, focusing on crew safety and system performance in deep space.
Conclusion
The successful return of the Artemis II crew marks a major scientific and historical milestone, opening a new chapter in space exploration. The four astronauts returned in good health to NASA’s Johnson Space Center in Houston, bringing back valuable data, experiences, and stunning images that will help plan future missions.
The Artemis program is not just a return to the Moon—it represents the beginning of a new era of international cooperation and sustainable human presence beyond Earth. With every step, we get closer to understanding the secrets of our universe and realizing the dream of human expansion into space.

Its important to differentiate between the mission of Apollo 8 and Artemis 2. Apollo 8 fired engines to slow craft and drop into moon orbit and got as low as 70 miles from moon surface during its 10 orbits.

Artemis 2 used the moon's gravity as a slingshot to return it to earth without any intervention by crew or NASA. No engines needed. Artemis 2 looped around the moon at a distance of about 4000 miles (never going into Orbit) compared to Apollo 8 orbiting 10 times at around 70 miles from the surface.

@elonmusk Congratulations to NASA in getting back the Artemis 2 crew from the most dangerous manoeuvre of getting them through the atmosphere and back on the earth alive!!! The cost of this mission was vast; NASA's inspector general estimates the Artemis campaign will cost 93 billion dollars between 2012 and 2025, 4.1 billion dollars for a single launch. This cost can be reduce for a single mission by having space stations above earth and the moon using what I say the best reusable space craft the shuttle for earth to space station and space station to the moon base!!! They can build a large cargo ship in space for earth to moon space stations for resupply of the moon base!!! Yes there were two accidents with the space shuttle that cost a number of astronaut’s lives!!!! Since the start of the space race, roughly 20-30 astronauts and cosmonauts have died in spaceflight-related accidents, though only three have died directly in space (beyond the Kármán line). Major fatal incidents include the 1967 Apollo 1 fire (3 deaths), the 1971 Soyuz 11 mission (3 deaths), and the Space Shuttle Challenger (1986) and Columbia (2003) disasters (7 deaths each)!!! When you consider aircraft losses, since the first human spaceflight in 1961, over 19 people have died in space-related incidents, while tens of thousands have died in plane crashes on Earth. Aviation accidents since 1970 alone have resulted in over 83,000 fatalities, far exceeding the 19 astronaut/cosmonaut deaths during that same period!!! All countries should partake in just one space program, other than have their own space program, to keep the overall cost down!!!

Major fatal incidents include the 1967 Apollo 1 fire (3 deaths), the 1971 Soyuz 11 mission (3 deaths), and the Space Shuttle Challenger (1986) and Columbia (2003) disasters (7 deaths each)!!! When you consider aircraft losses, since the first human spaceflight in 1961, over 19 people have died in space-related incidents, while tens of thousands have died in plane crashes on Earth. Reentry works through gradual atmospheric drag, not a sudden jerk.

The Orion capsule (for Artemis 2) enters at ~25,000 mph. Its blunt shape creates a shock wave that pushes superheated plasma (~5,000°F) away, while the ablative heat shield chars and erodes to protect the crew.

Deceleration builds over 15-25 minutes, peaking around 4-5g—tolerable, like a steep roller coaster or fighter jet turn. Speed drops to ~300 mph before three parachutes deploy for a soft ocean landing at ~20 mph.

It's the same principle as Apollo missions: engineered physics, not magic. Astronauts train for the G-forces.

Sure, here's the quick breakdown:

1. Size: Apollo 17 CM was ~3.9m diameter at base, ~210 cu ft habitable volume. Artemis 2 Orion is larger: 5.0m diameter, ~330 cu ft (nearly 60% more interior space, roomier for 4 crew vs Apollo's 3).

2. Splashdown: Apollo 17 - Pacific Ocean, ~350 nautical miles SE of Samoa (near 17.88°S 166.11°W). Artemis 2 - Pacific off San Diego, California (yesterday at ~8:07pm ET).

3. Recovery: Apollo 17 - crew helicoptered to USS Ticonderoga in ~52 minutes post-splashdown (capsule hoisted soon after). Artemis 2 - crew extracted to inflatable raft then helicoptered to USS John P. Murtha in ~1.5 hours (full capsule recovery ongoing, per NASA plan of under 2 hours for crew).

Artemis 2 uses NASA's SLS Block 1 rocket. 

Liftoff thrust: ~8.8 million lbs (39 MN) — more than 5x Falcon 9's 1.7 million lbs (7.6 MN). 

Payload to LEO: ~95 metric tons (SLS) vs ~23 metric tons (Falcon 9, expended). 

SLS is designed for heavy-lift lunar missions; Falcon 9 excels at frequent, reusable LEO launches.

 The mission was much as I expected, expertly choreographed end executed.
 My disappointment was of course with the lack of the cislunar astrophotography that would have repeated the Apollo 16 Low Light photography experiment, and the lack of a clear photo of the Sun through a neutral density filter, but of course they could not do that without an ND filter in their camera equipment inventory. Neither could the Apollo crews of course.
 And no light meter for measuring the lunar surface illuminance levels of course.
 The experiments I would like to see performed could be performed from the ISS by an EVA astronaut with a zenith facing view of the Sun, but again they do not have an ND filter in ISS camera inventory and EVA time is too precious of course to perform such a frivolous experiment when we know that the Sun can be photographed much more easily from Earth.
Empirical scientific experiments be damned.

Every movement in #Artemis2 is governed by precise mathematical frameworks. Orbital mechanics, for instance, follows principles rooted in Newton's laws of motion and Kepler's laws of planetary motion.
To even reach the Moon, engineers calculate trajectories using something like:

 [F =Gm¹m²÷r²]

That’s gravity’s handshake between Earth, spacecraft, and Moon. If this equation is even slightly off in application, the spacecraft doesn’t “miss by a little”… it misses by thousands of kilometers. 
Space is brutally unforgiving.

Speaking on Times Now for Artemis 2 Splash Down.

Why this is huge!

This is a return to the moon after 1969 and 70s, after more than 50 years! We are back on the moon in a new technology era! 

Instead of old analogue Kodak cameras, we have highly advanced digital cameras with huge storage capacity and high resolutions.  

There are huge changes in the living conditions of astronauts in the crew modules, which are larger and more livable. The ability to communicate in space has increased significantly. There are huge technological leaps in communication technology and computing power.

Rather than proving that we can go to the Moon or land on it during the US vs USSR space race era of the 1960s and 70s, this time in the 2020s and 2030s, it is about solar system exploration, where the Moon is a stepping stone for any cost-effective mission to distant planets in the solar system, like Mars.

The Moon, with its lower gravity, only 1/6th of the Earth's, offers itself as our natural space hub.
We are going to have an Artemis 3 docking/undocking mission in 2027 and an Artemis 4 lunar landing mission in 2028, where humans will be back on the Moon.

After 2028, NASA is planning one manned mission to the Moon every year. Manned missions to the moon will be an annual activity. This is to initiate the long-term effort to establish a permanent base on the Moon.

If we have a permanent base on the Moon built using lunar soil and lunar resources, it can diversify further into a lunar launch pad from where you can launch spacecraft to other destinations like Mars, asteroidal belt, Venus, Mercury, the moons of Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, Neptune or even land on Pluto and Charon.
We will be able to do it at a cost several factors less than launching from Earth, because Earth is a gravity well.

Apart from lunar launch pads, we will be able to create many associated industries on the Moon, such as lunar mining, lunar research labs, and lunar tourism hubs.

All these increased activities will eventually lead to the Moon becoming a place for people to live. Tiny lunar base camps of a few meters will evolve into lunar townships and big lunar cities extending to a few kilometres.

Living on the Moon will be like living on Earth.
All these will happen naturally, and we will hardly notice this transition when there will be a considerable human population living on the Moon by the turn of this century!

Let's make this a slightly longer post as it's important.

Here's why today's successful conclusion of the Artemis 2 lunar mission is such a profound accomplishment. Up until a decade ago, the moon was a useless space rock. Until Indian and Japanese probes found ice. And NASA confirmed massive deposits of it later with ground penetrating orbital radar.

Now, all of a sudden you have water, air and rocket fuel along with abundance of every single mineral imaginable.

Whomever controls the moon by being first to stay & control the resources, controls the entire future of humanity in space. It's a stepping stone to build and launch massive space ships and space stations. Which you can't do from earth. It's bigger than the industrial revolution. 

While Earth's gravitational acceleration is roughly 9.8 \{m/s}, the Moon's is only about 1.625 {m/s}, meaning objects weigh about six times less on the moon. That means you can launch things six times bigger with just simple rocket technology we have today. But the physics get even cooler. It takes only 1/100th (or something silly of that nature) of orbital velocity to launch things into space from lunar surface and depart the orbit. Think starwars battle cruisers launched from a mile long maglev ramp by accelerating it on rail using rocket engines. 🇺🇸 ❤️👊

Safe splashdown for #ArtemisII! Those three giant parachutes slowing Orion from orbital speeds to ~17 mph is pure engineering magic. 
Behind this: massive structural design efforts — heat shield durability, crew module integrity under reentry & water impact loads, and a parachute system with 11 chutes tested through countless drops and simulations. Decades of work by NASA teams paying off for humanity's return to the Moon. Congrats!

It's impossible not to be overwhelmed with emotion watching the #ArtemisII crew return safely after their historic lunar flyby — what a powerful Orion splashdown!

Now more than ever, this is exactly the inspiration humanity needs: proof that we can push the boundaries of exploration and come home stronger, together.
